overall_summary: L. Lynne Kiesling, Ph.D., applies transaction-cost and regulatory economics to argue for a modular, principles-based federal framework for large-load interconnection. She contends that data center flexibility is undervalued, co-location is a rational response to high transaction costs, and the ANOPR's 100% participant-funding proposal rests on flawed cost-allocation methodology. She recommends reform grounded in marginal-cost principles and expanded market participation for flexible resources.

quotes.text: The rapid growth of AI data center load presents a critical coordination challenge due to the temporal mismatch between rapid data center deployment (18–24 months) and slow grid infrastructure expansion (7–10 years).
quotes.concern: Pacing gap between data center construction and grid buildout creates coordination failure

quotes.text: Modern data centers possess significant latent flexibility through workload shifting, UPS-based frequency response, and hybrid on-site generation.
quotes.concern: Data centers have underused flexibility that current rules ignore

quotes.text: Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.
quotes.concern: Static-load treatment in interconnection rules wastes available flexibility

quotes.text: Legacy interconnection rules create high asset specificity and hold-up risks, driving developers toward inefficient bypass strategies.
quotes.concern: Legacy rules raise transaction costs and push developers toward workarounds

quotes.text: Co-location and hybrid configurations are rational economic responses to these high transaction costs ("make vs. buy" decisions) and should be facilitated rather than penalized by extending generator-style interconnection regimes to loads.
quotes.concern: Co-location is economically rational and should be accommodated, not penalized

quotes.text: The ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades relies on flawed embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal impacts.
quotes.concern: Proposed full participant funding is based on flawed cost methodology

quotes.text: Efficient cost recovery should instead follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles, allocating fixed costs inversely to elasticity.
quotes.concern: Cost allocation should use marginal-cost principles, not embedded-cost averages

quotes.text: Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.
quotes.concern: Full upgrade cost assignment to elastic loads drives inefficient bypass of the grid

quotes.text: The Commission should establish a federal framework that lowers barriers to flexible resources, modernizes market participation rules for technologies like thermal energy storage (TES), and reforms cost allocation to reflect marginal impacts.
quotes.concern: Calls for a federal framework enabling flexibility and marginal-cost-based cost allocation

bins.name: Pacing mismatch study need
bins.description: The filer identifies a coordination failure: data centers deploy in 18 to 24 months while grid infrastructure takes 7 to 10 years. This structural lag is the foundation for recommending a federal framework rather than leaving the problem to case-by-case interconnection.

bins.name: Treat flexibility as system value
bins.description: The filer argues that data centers hold significant latent flexibility through workload shifting, UPS response, and on-site generation, and that current rules ignore this. She supports modernizing market participation rules so these capabilities reduce upgrade costs and system stress.

bins.name: Reform upgrade cost allocation
bins.description: The filer opposes 100% participant funding as grounded in flawed embedded-cost methodology that ignores marginal impacts. She advocates Ramsey-Boiteux cost allocation, warning that full cost assignment to elastic large loads will push them toward grid defection.

bins.name: Facilitate co-location economically
bins.description: The filer frames co-location as a rational make-vs-buy response to high transaction costs in legacy interconnection rules. She supports extending generator-style interconnection regimes to loads rather than penalizing co-location arrangements.

bins.name: Elastic loads and grid defection risk
bins.description: The filer notes that large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, making full upgrade cost assignment counterproductive. This frames the proximate-load question in terms of economic efficiency rather than geography.

bins.name: Participant funding methodology flawed
bins.description: The filer opposes the ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding, arguing it relies on embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal cost impacts and misalign incentives for large elastic loads.

bins.name: Deployment vs. grid buildout lag
bins.description: The structural mismatch between fast data center deployment and slow grid expansion is the organizing problem the filing addresses. The filer uses it to justify a modular, principles-based federal response.

bins.name: Undervalued data center flexibility
bins.description: The filer argues current rules treat large loads as static, overlooking workload shifting, UPS frequency response, and hybrid generation. Recognizing and enabling this flexibility could materially reduce infrastructure costs.

bins.name: Legacy rules raise transaction costs
bins.description: Applying transaction-cost economics, the filer argues that legacy interconnection rules create asset specificity and hold-up risks that push developers toward bypass strategies. Co-location is a rational economic response, not an abuse.

bins.name: Marginal vs. embedded cost principles
bins.description: The filer argues cost allocation should follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles rather than embedded-cost averages. Assigning full upgrade costs to highly elastic loads encourages grid defection and is economically inefficient.

bins.name: Market rules for flexible technologies
bins.description: The filer calls for modernized market participation rules covering technologies like thermal energy storage so that demand-side flexibility can be valued and compensated within wholesale markets.
